critique.summary: The article introduces Wander, a decentralized tool that allows users to explore a network of personal websites through interconnected consoles. Users can set up their own Wander console by downloading specific files and joining the Wander community to browse random websites within the network.
mainPoints: Wander is a decentralized browsing tool enabling users to explore random websites from the Wander community.
Users can set up their own Wander console by downloading a ZIP file containing index.html and wander.js and placing them in a specific directory on their website.
Once set up, users can share their Wander console link to join the network and allow others to wander to their site.
The Wander community consists of individuals maintaining personal websites who participate by linking their consoles.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about Wander being a decentralized tool for browsing personal websites and the setup instructions are consistent with information available on the official code repository at codeberg.org/susam/wander. The description aligns with the concept of decentralized web browsing and personal website networks, which is a recognized approach in web development communities. However, the article does not cite multiple sources or provide external perspectives, limiting its journalistic reliability but not necessarily the accuracy of the technical content.
considerations: The article does not discuss potential security or privacy implications of using Wander consoles.
There is no mention of how the network handles malicious or inappropriate content within the decentralized system.
The scalability and user adoption of the Wander network are not addressed.
No comparison is made with other decentralized web browsing tools or protocols.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the launch of Wander, a decentralized tool inspired by Kagi Small Web and reminiscent of webrings and StumbleUpon. Users appreciate its simplicity and potential for curated discovery but raise questions about its workflow, curation, and technical limitations.

comments.author: susam
comments.text:
Hello HN!

This tool is inspired by Kagi Small Web (recent thread: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47410542). A common concern raised here is that Kagi Small Web currently accepts only blogs, comics and YouTube channels. It does not accept arbitrary small websites. That limitation motivated me to build Wander.

Wander is fully decentralised. Anyone can host it on their own website. It consists of just two files: an `index.html` for the Wander console and a `wander.js` where you link to other Wander consoles. It is a bit like a webring, but more flexible. Each console can link to any number of sites and other consoles.

There is no server-side code, no database, nothing to install. If you have a website, you can set it up by uploading just two files. In fact, you can host it on GitHub Pages or Codeberg Pages too.

If you like the idea, please join the network. I would love to see it grow.

More details about how it works and how to set it up here: https://codeberg.org/susam/wander#readme
comments.analysis: As the creator, this comment provides accurate and detailed information about Wander's design and goals.

comments.author: InexSquirrel
comments.text:
Very cool. Reminds me of stumbleupon, which I lost many hours to back in the day.

Curated discovery is one of biggest gripes with modern platforms like youtube - discovering something truly new and outside of your normal interests is really difficult, and the same goes for the web. If you have a topic you want to explore it's fine, but finding random things you'd never have thought of yourself is much harder.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully highlights the value of curated discovery, aligning well with the purpose of Wander.

comments.author: punknight
comments.text: I love this as a concept. The wander button is great, but it still needs some curating to decide what pages you like, and getting to the actual content. I guess I'd like to know the workflow moving forward? Just re-download the repo every couple weeks, and diff to see what new sites are on the list?
comments.analysis: Raises a valid concern about the need for curation and clarity on the workflow for updating the linked sites.

comments.author: dgb23
comments.text: I want to like it, but I don't fully understand why one wouldn't just put a bunch of links on a /wander page and maybe randomize the order?
comments.analysis: Questions the unique value proposition of Wander compared to simpler link collections, prompting useful discussion.

comments.author: deanebarker
comments.text: Love this, but I need it to allow me to break the frame. I found some neat sites, but I need a button to open them in a new tab, frame-free, if I want to.
comments.analysis: Provides practical feedback on usability improvements that could enhance user experience.

comments.author: superkuh
comments.text: It would be cool if the javascript were re-written a bit to only use standard long existing javascript features so one didn't have to run a modern corporate browser to use the "wander" functionality. It did not work in my browser even with JS enabled.
comments.analysis: Offers a technical critique about compatibility that could broaden accessibility.

comments.author: xnorswap
comments.text: So is this web rings all over again?
comments.analysis: Draws a historical parallel that is relevant but somewhat simplistic.

comments.author: warkdarrior
comments.text: This will get really fun when influencers get a hold of the idea and start connecting themselves into the Wander community.
comments.analysis: Speculative and somewhat emotional about potential social dynamics, less grounded in current facts.

comments.author: shevy-java
comments.text:
I don't know how useful this is, but I am getting
tired of Google and co ruining the world wide web
how it once was. Something has to be done. I have
no idea whether this here can be of help or not
but the more people think about this, the better.
Otherwise the quality will continue to degrade.
comments.analysis: Expresses a common sentiment about centralized platforms but is vague about Wander's actual impact.

comments.author: desireco42
comments.text:
Ahhh... this is like my OneRandomSite.com from... 2006 or so... nice.

I think we need things like this. To be reintroduced over and over.
comments.analysis: Provides historical context supporting the idea that such discovery tools are repeatedly valuable.
